guide.title: Complete AWS Guide for Data Engineers

topics.name: What is AWS
topics.explanation: Amazon Web Services (AWS) is the world's most comprehensive and broadly adopted cloud platform, launched in 2006. It offers 200+ fully featured services from data centers globally. AWS follows a pay-as-you-go model — you pay only for what you use, with no upfront commitment. It powers companies from startups to enterprises including Netflix, Airbnb, NASA, and the CIA.
key_benefits: Agility — provision resources in minutes, not weeks
Elasticity — scale up/down based on demand automatically
Cost savings — trade capital expense for variable expense
Global reach — deploy in 30+ geographic regions worldwide
Security — AWS meets 143 security standards and compliance certifications
Breadth — 200+ services covering compute, storage, AI, IoT, and more

topics.name: Global Infrastructure (Regions, AZs, Edge Locations)
topics.explanation: AWS global infrastructure is the foundation of the cloud. It is architected for maximum availability, performance, and security. By understanding the hierarchy of Regions, Availability Zones, and Points of Presence, data engineers can design highly resilient and low-latency data platforms that scale across the globe.
topics.diagram:
graph TD
    A[AWS Global Infrastructure] --> B[Regions]
    B --> C[Availability Zones]
    C --> D[Data Centers]
    A --> E[Points of Presence]
    E --> F[Edge Locations]
    E --> G[Regional Edge Caches]
topics.pro_tip: When choosing a Region, always consider the 'Big Four' factors: Data Sovereignty (Legal), Latency (User Experience), Service Availability (Feature sets), and Cost (Pricing varies by region).

components.description: A Region is a physical location around the world where AWS clusters data centers. Each AWS Region is designed to be completely isolated from the other AWS Regions. This achieves the greatest fault tolerance and stability.
components.use_case: Select a region closest to your primary data sources to minimize ingest latency.

components.description: An AZ is one or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity in an AWS Region. AZs give customers the ability to operate production applications and databases that are more highly available, fault tolerant, and scalable than would be possible from a single data center.
components.use_case: Standard practice for production data platforms is to deploy across at least 3 AZs.

components.description: To deliver content to end users with lower latency, Amazon CloudFront uses a global network of 450+ Points of Presence (400+ Edge locations and 13 regional edge caches) in 90+ cities across 48 countries.
components.use_case: Reducing latency for large file downloads or streaming media via CloudFront CDN.

best_practices: Always design for Multi-AZ deployments to ensure HA (High Availability).
Use Regional Edge Caches for large content objects that are not accessed frequently.
Consider AWS Local Zones for single-digit millisecond latency requirements in specific metro areas.

topics.name: Shared Responsibility Model
topics.explanation: AWS and the customer share security responsibilities. AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud (infrastructure). Customers are responsible for security IN the cloud (what they put there).
aws_responsibility: Managed service software (RDS OS patching, Lambda runtime)
customer_responsibility: Data encryption at rest and in transit
IAM — who has access to what

examples.customer_handles: OS patching, application security, security groups, data encryption

examples.aws_handles: OS patching, database engine patching, hardware
examples.customer_handles: Database schema, user access, data encryption, network access (security groups)
Bucket policies, IAM permissions, encryption settings, public access settings
Function code, IAM execution role, VPC config, environment variables

topics.name: AWS Well-Architected Framework
topics.explanation: Six pillars that help build secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure on AWS.

key_practices: Protect data in transit and at rest
Stop spending money on undifferentiated heavy lifting

topics.name: AWS CLI & SDK Setup
topics.explanation: AWS CLI and SDKs are the primary programmatic interfaces to AWS services.

cli_setup.install:
# macOS:
brew install awscli

# Linux:
curl 'https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip' -o 'awscliv2.zip'
unzip awscliv2.zip && sudo ./aws/install

# Verify:
aws --version
cli_setup.configure:
aws configure
# Prompts for:
# AWS Access Key ID: AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
# AWS Secret Access Key: wJalrXUtn...
# Default region: us-east-1
# Default output format: json
cli_setup.multiple_profiles:
aws configure --profile dev
aws configure --profile prod

# Use profile:
aws s3 ls --profile prod
export AWS_PROFILE=prod
useful_commands: aws sts get-caller-identity          # Who am I?
aws ec2 describe-instances            # List EC2 instances
aws iam list-users                    # List IAM users
aws cloudformation list-stacks        # List CF stacks

sdk_python_boto3.example:
import boto3

# Create S3 client
s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='us-east-1')

# List buckets
response = s3.list_buckets()
for bucket in response['Buckets']:
    print(bucket['Name'])

# Upload file
s3.upload_file('local_file.csv', 'my-bucket', 'data/file.csv')

# Use resource (higher-level)
s3_resource = boto3.resource('s3')
bucket = s3_resource.Bucket('my-bucket')
for obj in bucket.objects.all():
    print(obj.key)

topics.name: Users, Groups, Roles
topics.explanation: IAM (Identity and Access Management) is the security heart of AWS. It allows you to manage access to AWS services and resources securely. By using IAM, you can create and manage AWS users and groups, and use permissions to allow and deny their access to AWS resources. The core philosophy of IAM is the 'Principle of Least Privilege'—giving only the minimum access required to perform a task.
topics.diagram:
graph TD
    A[IAM Identity] --> B[Policies]
    B --> C[Allow/Deny Access]
    C --> D[AWS Resources]
    subgraph Identities
    A1[Users] -- belong to --> A2[Groups]
    A3[Roles] -- assumed by --> A4[Services/Users]
    end
topics.pro_tip: Never use the AWS Root user for daily tasks. Create an IAM user with Administrator permissions for administrative tasks and use Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for all human users.
topics.warning: IAM Access Keys are permanent. If they are leaked (e.g., committed to GitHub), attackers can gain full access to your account. Prefer IAM Roles for applications as they use temporary, rotating credentials.

users.description: IAM Users represent a person or application that interacts with AWS. Each user has permanent credentials (access keys or password).
users.best_practice: Use IAM users only for humans needing console access. Use roles for applications and services.
users.create_user_cli:
aws iam create-user --user-name john-doe
aws iam create-login-profile --user-name john-doe --password 'TempPass123!' --password-reset-required
aws iam create-access-key --user-name john-doe

groups.description: Groups are collections of IAM users. Attach policies to groups, add users to groups. Users inherit group permissions.

example.cli:
aws iam create-group --group-name DataEngineers
aws iam attach-group-policy --group-name DataEngineers --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3FullAccess
aws iam add-user-to-group --group-name DataEngineers --user-name john-doe

roles.description: Roles are AWS identities with permissions that can be assumed temporarily. No permanent credentials — temporary security tokens issued via STS. Used for: EC2 instances accessing S3, Lambda functions, cross-account access, federated users.
roles.example_ec2_role:
# Create role for EC2 to access S3:
aws iam create-role \
  --role-name EC2-S3-ReadRole \
  --assume-role-policy-document '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {"Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"},
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  }'

aws iam attach-role-policy \
  --role-name EC2-S3-ReadRole \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess

topics.name: Policies (Inline, Managed, Customer-Managed)
topics.explanation: IAM policies are JSON documents that define permissions. They specify what actions are allowed or denied on which resources.

policy_types.description: Pre-built by AWS. Automatically updated when new services launch. Examples: AmazonS3FullAccess, AmazonEC2ReadOnlyAccess, AdministratorAccess
policy_types.drawback: Often too permissive — grants more than needed

policy_types.description: Created and managed by you. Reusable across multiple identities. Version-controlled.
policy_types.use_when: Custom permission requirements, fine-grained access control

policy_types.description: Embedded directly into a single user, group, or role. Deleted when identity is deleted.
policy_types.use_when: Permissions that must not be accidentally attached to another identity
policy_types.drawback: Not reusable, harder to manage at scale

elements.description: ARN of resources the action applies to

practices.practice: Enable MFA for root and all users
practices.reason: Root account compromise is catastrophic. MFA prevents credential theft attacks.

practices.practice: Never use root account for daily tasks
practices.reason: Root has unlimited permissions — create admin IAM user instead
Grant minimum permissions needed. Start restrictive and loosen as needed.

practices.practice: Use roles instead of access keys for services
practices.reason: Access keys can be leaked. Roles provide temporary, auto-rotating credentials.
Leaked old keys are compromised. Rotate every 90 days.

practices.practice: Use groups for permissions, not users directly
practices.reason: Easier to manage — change group policy once vs updating many users
Track all API calls — who did what and when
Automatically identifies resources shared with external entities
Safely delegate IAM permissions without escalation risk

topics.name: Temporary Credentials (STS, AssumeRole)
topics.explanation: AWS Security Token Service (STS) issues temporary security credentials (access key ID, secret access key, session token) that expire automatically. The foundation of roles and cross-account access.

key_operations.description: Assume an IAM role and get temporary credentials. Used for cross-account access, EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution.
key_operations.example:
import boto3

# Assume a role in another account
sts = boto3.client('sts')
response = sts.assume_role(
    RoleArn='arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/DataAccessRole',
    RoleSessionName='MySession',
    DurationSeconds=3600  # 1 hour (default)
)

credentials = response['Credentials']
# Use temporary credentials:
s3 = boto3.client(
    's3',
    aws_access_key_id=credentials['AccessKeyId'],
    aws_secret_access_key=credentials['SecretAccessKey'],
    aws_session_token=credentials['SessionToken']
)

key_operations.description: Get temporary credentials for the current IAM user, optionally with MFA.
Exchange web identity token (Google, Facebook, Amazon Cognito) for AWS credentials.
key_operations.use_case: Mobile apps, web apps authenticating with social providers

cross_account_example.scenario: Account A (data platform, 111111111111) needs to read from Account B's S3 bucket (222222222222)
cross_account_example.account_a_usage:
# In Account A, assume Account B's role:
response = sts.assume_role(
    RoleArn='arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/S3ReadRole',
    RoleSessionName='CrossAccountDataAccess'
)

topics.name: EC2 Instance Types & Families
topics.explanation: EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) provides resizable virtual machines in the cloud. Instance types are optimized for different use cases.

instance_families.use_case: Web servers, small databases, dev/test, microservices
Batch processing, ML inference, high-performance web servers, video encoding
In-memory databases (Redis, SAP HANA), large dataset processing, Spark executors
NoSQL databases (Cassandra), data warehousing, Hadoop HDFS, log processing
Deep learning training/inference, HPC, graphics rendering
Up to 40% better price/performance than x86. Web servers, containerized apps, Java apps

topics.name: EC2 Pricing Models
topics.explanation: AWS offers multiple pricing options for EC2 with significant cost differences.

pricing_models.description: Pay per second/hour with no commitment. Most expensive per hour.
pricing_models.use_case: Unpredictable workloads, short-term testing, spiky traffic

pricing_models.description: Commit to 1 or 3 years for a specific instance type. Large discount.
types: Convertible RI (change instance family/OS, less discount)

pricing_models.description: Commit to a consistent amount of compute usage ($/hour) for 1-3 years. More flexible than RIs.
types: Compute Savings Plans (applies to EC2, Lambda, Fargate)
pricing_models.use_case: Organizations with mixed workloads wanting flexibility

pricing_models.description: Use AWS spare capacity at up to 90% discount. Can be interrupted with 2-minute warning.
pricing_models.use_case: Fault-tolerant batch jobs, ML training, data processing, CI/CD
pricing_models.drawback: Instance can be reclaimed by AWS with 2-minute notice
pricing_models.solutions: Use Spot with checkpointing. Use Spot Fleets mixing multiple instance types. Use interruption handlers.

pricing_models.description: Physical server dedicated to your use. Required for software licenses tied to physical cores/sockets.
pricing_models.use_case: BYOL (Bring Your Own License) for Windows Server, SQL Server, Oracle

topics.name: Auto Scaling Groups
topics.explanation: Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs) automatically adjust the number of EC2 instances based on demand. Maintains minimum/maximum/desired capacity. Integrates with ELB for health checks.

key_params.description: Minimum number of instances (never go below)
Target number of instances (ASG tries to maintain)
Defines instance configuration (AMI, type, security groups)

scaling_policies.description: Maintain a metric at a target value (e.g., CPU at 60%)
scaling_policies.example: Keep average CPU utilization at 60%. If CPU rises to 80%, add instances. If drops to 40%, remove instances.

scaling_policies.description: Add/remove instances in steps based on CloudWatch alarm breach size
scaling_policies.example: CPU 60-80%: add 1 instance. CPU 80-100%: add 3 instances.

scaling_policies.description: Pre-schedule capacity changes at known times
scaling_policies.example: Scale up to 10 instances every Monday 8am. Scale down to 2 instances at 8pm.

scaling_policies.description: ML-based future demand forecasting. Proactively scales before load arrives.
scaling_policies.use_case: Regular traffic patterns (business hours, daily cycles)

topics.example_cloudformation:
Resources:
  MyASG:
    Type: AWS::AutoScaling::AutoScalingGroup
    Properties:
      MinSize: 2
      MaxSize: 20
      DesiredCapacity: 4
      LaunchTemplate:
        LaunchTemplateId: !Ref MyLaunchTemplate
        Version: !GetAtt MyLaunchTemplate.LatestVersionNumber
      TargetGroupARNs: [!Ref MyTargetGroup]
      AvailabilityZones: [us-east-1a, us-east-1b, us-east-1c]
      HealthCheckType: ELB
      HealthCheckGracePeriod: 300

topics.name: AWS Lambda
topics.explanation: Lambda is a serverless compute service that runs code in response to events without managing servers. Pay per invocation and execution duration (in milliseconds). Automatically scales from 0 to thousands of concurrent executions.

key_concepts.description: Lambda runs in an isolated container. Containers are reused ('warm start') or created fresh ('cold start'). Cold start latency: 100ms-1s depending on runtime and memory.
Lambda scales by creating new execution environments for concurrent invocations. Default: 1000 concurrent executions per account per region. Can be increased via quota request.
key_concepts.reserved_concurrency: Guarantee minimum concurrency for a function. Prevents function from consuming all regional concurrency.
key_concepts.provisioned_concurrency: Pre-warm execution environments to eliminate cold starts for latency-critical functions.

example.s3_trigger_python:
import json, boto3

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    # Process S3 upload event
    for record in event['Records']:
        bucket = record['s3']['bucket']['name']
        key    = record['s3']['object']['key']
        print(f'New file: s3://{bucket}/{key}')

        # Process the file
        s3 = boto3.client('s3')
        response = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
        content = response['Body'].read().decode('utf-8')

        # Transform and save to processed bucket
        processed = content.upper()  # Simple transform
        s3.put_object(
            Bucket='processed-bucket',
            Key=f'processed/{key}',
            Body=processed
        )

    return {'statusCode': 200, 'body': 'Processed'}

configuration.layers: Share code/libraries across functions (up to 5 layers, 250MB unzipped)

topics.pricing: First 1M requests/month FREE, then $0.20 per 1M requests. Compute: $0.0000166667 per GB-second.
drawbacks: Cold start latency for latency-sensitive apps
15-minute max timeout — not for long-running jobs
10GB max memory — not for large in-memory datasets
solutions: Use Provisioned Concurrency for cold start elimination
Use ECS/Fargate for long-running jobs
Split large jobs into Lambda chains using Step Functions

topics.name: ECS (Elastic Container Service)
topics.explanation: Fully managed container orchestration service for Docker containers. Two launch types: EC2 (you manage instances) and Fargate (serverless — AWS manages infrastructure).

key_concepts.description: Blueprint defining how a container runs: Docker image, CPU/memory, environment variables, port mappings, IAM role, log configuration.
Running instance of a Task Definition. Can be one-time (batch job) or long-running (service).
Maintains desired count of tasks running. Integrates with ALB for load balancing. Handles task failure replacement.
Logical grouping of tasks and services. EC2 clusters: you manage the instances. Fargate clusters: serverless.

topics.name: EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)
topics.explanation: Fully managed Kubernetes control plane. AWS manages the Kubernetes masters (API server, etcd). You manage (or AWS manages with Fargate) the worker nodes.

components.description: Managed by AWS across multiple AZs. Includes API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager.
EC2 instances running kubelet. Options: Managed Node Groups (AWS manages), Self-managed nodes, Fargate (serverless).
CoreDNS, kube-proxy, Amazon VPC CNI, EBS CSI Driver — managed by AWS, kept updated.

topics.use_case: Containerized data pipelines, microservices, when Kubernetes expertise exists in team
topics.vs_ecs: EKS: standard Kubernetes — portable, more features, higher complexity. ECS: AWS-native, simpler, tighter AWS integration, less overhead.

topics.name: AWS Batch
topics.explanation: Fully managed batch processing service. Automatically provisions compute resources (EC2 or Fargate) for batch jobs. No servers to manage. Jobs defined as Docker containers.

key_concepts.description: Template for a batch job — Docker image, CPU/memory, retry strategy, environment variables.
Jobs submitted to a queue with priority. Multiple queues can share a compute environment.
Pool of managed EC2 or Fargate resources. AWS Batch automatically scales up/down based on job queue.

flow: Submits batch job to AWS Batch job queue
AWS Batch launches EC2 Spot instances (cost-optimized)
Docker container runs Spark/Python ETL processing 500GB data
S3 output files trigger next pipeline stage
example_use_case.cost_advantage: Pay only during job execution. Spot instances reduce cost by 70-80% vs On-Demand.

topics.name: S3 (Simple Storage Service)
topics.explanation:
### Master Level Deep Dive into Amazon S3

Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) is the **foundational storage layer** of the AWS cloud. For data engineers, it serves as the primary **Data Lake** repository, providing virtually unlimited scalability, industry-leading durability, and high availability.

#### 1. Core Concepts & Architecture
*   **Buckets**: Fundamental containers for data. Bucket names are **globally unique** across all AWS accounts.
*   **Objects**: The entities stored in S3. An object consists of the **Data** (file), **Key** (the full path/name), and **Metadata** (name-value pairs).
*   **Keys**: The unique identifier for an object. S3 is a 'flat' storage system, but we simulate folders using delimiters (usually `/`).

#### 2. Durability vs. Availability
*   **Durability (11 Nines)**: Designed to prevent data loss. 99.999999999% durability means if you store 10 million objects, you might lose one every 10,000 years.
*   **Availability**: Designed for system uptime. Varies by storage class (e.g., 99.99% for Standard, 99.9% for Standard-IA).

#### 3. Security & Access Control
S3 follows a **'Default Deny'** policy. Access can be granted via:
1.  **IAM Policies**: Attached to Users/Roles (Identity-based).
2.  **Bucket Policies**: Attached to the bucket (Resource-based). Best for cross-account access or public access rules.
3.  **Access Control Lists (ACLs)**: Legacy method, mostly disabled in modern architectures.
4.  **S3 Access Points**: Simplifies managing access for large shared datasets by creating unique hostnames for different applications.

#### 4. Performance Optimization
*   **Multipart Uploads**: Required for files > 5GB, recommended for > 100MB. Improves upload speed by parallelizing parts.
*   **S3 Select**: Retrieve only a subset of data from an object (CSV, JSON, Parquet) using SQL-like queries, reducing data transfer and latency.
*   **Transfer Acceleration**: Uses AWS Edge Locations to speed up long-distance uploads via the optimized AWS private network.
topics.diagram:
graph TD
    A[Data Source] -->|Upload| B[S3 Bucket]
    B -->|Lifecycle Policy| C{Storage Classes}
    C --> D[S3 Standard]
    C --> E[S3 Intelligent-Tiering]
    C --> F[S3 Glacier Deep Archive]
    B -->|Event Notification| G[AWS Lambda]
    B -->|Analytic Access| H[Amazon Athena]
topics.pro_tip: **VPC Endpoints are non-negotiable for Production.** When running Glue jobs or EC2 instances, use a Gateway VPC Endpoint for S3 to avoid routing traffic over the public internet. This increases security and eliminates Data Transfer charges.
topics.warning: **Block Public Access is your best friend.** Always enable 'Block All Public Access' at the account level unless you are explicitly hosting a public static website. S3 data leaks are almost always due to misconfigured public permissions.

components.name: Strong Consistency
components.explanation: Since December 2020, S3 provides **Strong Read-After-Write Consistency** for all operations (PUT, DELETE, LIST). Previously, it was eventually consistent for overwrites and deletes.
components.use_case: Critical for data pipelines where a 'Write' must be immediately followed by a 'Read' (e.g., Spark jobs).

components.name: S3 Versioning
components.explanation: Keeps multiple versions of an object in the same bucket. Protects against accidental deletes or overwrites.
components.use_case: Recovery from human error or malicious deletions.

key_features: Unlimited storage capacity
Object size: 0 bytes to 5TB
11 nines of durability (data replicated across minimum 3 AZs)
Global namespace — bucket names must be globally unique
Strong read-after-write consistency (since 2020)
Versioning support
Event notifications
Static website hosting

topics.name: S3 Storage Classes
topics.explanation: S3 offers a wide range of storage classes that you can choose from based on the data access, resiliency, and cost requirements of your workloads. Data engineers must master these classes to optimize the 'Total Cost of Ownership' (TCO) for data lakes.

components.name: S3 Standard
components.explanation: The default storage class for frequently accessed data. Provides low latency and high throughput. Replicated across at least 3 Availability Zones.
components.use_case: Active data lake ingest, staging areas, and frequently queried datasets.

components.name: S3 Intelligent-Tiering
components.explanation: The only cloud storage class that delivers automatic cost savings by moving data between two access tiers (frequent and infrequent) when access patterns change.
components.use_case: Datasets with unknown or unpredictable access patterns.

components.name: S3 Glacier Deep Archive
components.explanation: The lowest-cost storage class in AWS. Designed for long-term retention of data that is accessed once or twice a year.
components.use_case: Regulatory compliance data and long-term backups where 12-hour retrieval is acceptable.

storage_classes.use_case: Frequently accessed data — active data lake, current month data, dashboards
Unknown or changing access patterns — auto-moves objects between tiers
Infrequently accessed but needs fast retrieval — 30-90 day old data
Reproducible infrequent data — secondary backups, derived data
Archived data accessed once a quarter — historical analytics
7-10 year regulatory compliance archives. Rarely if ever accessed.

topics.name: S3 Lifecycle Policies
topics.explanation: Automate moving objects between storage classes or deleting them based on age or other criteria.

example.cost_impact: Moving 1TB from Standard to Glacier Deep Archive: $23/month → $1/month (95% savings)

topics.name: S3 Encryption
topics.explanation: S3 supports multiple encryption options for data at rest.

encryption_types.description: AWS manages encryption keys using AES-256. Default encryption since 2023.
encryption_types.use_case: Default — all data encrypted, minimal management overhead

encryption_types.description: Use AWS KMS to manage encryption keys. Audit trail in CloudTrail. Can use customer-managed keys.
encryption_types.use_case: Compliance requirements needing key audit trail, customer-managed keys
encryption_types.drawback: Additional KMS API cost. KMS throttling can impact S3 throughput at high request rates.

encryption_types.description: You provide the encryption key in each request. AWS encrypts/decrypts but never stores the key.
encryption_types.use_case: When you must control the encryption key entirely (key never leaves your control)

encryption_types.description: Encrypt data BEFORE uploading to S3. AWS never sees unencrypted data.

topics.name: S3 Access Control
topics.explanation: Multiple layers of access control for S3. They work in combination — a request must pass all applicable checks.

access_control_layers.description: Attached to IAM identities. Controls what an identity can do in S3.
access_control_layers.example: Allow user to read from specific bucket prefix

access_control_layers.description: JSON policies attached to S3 bucket. Controls who can access the bucket and what they can do. Can grant cross-account access.
Account or bucket level setting to prevent any public access regardless of bucket policies. DEFAULT: all block settings ENABLED.
access_control_layers.best_practice: Keep Block Public Access enabled at account level. Only disable for specific buckets that need public access (static websites).

access_control_layers.description: Legacy mechanism. Generally disabled with Object Ownership=BucketOwnerEnforced. Use bucket policies instead.
access_control_layers.recommendation: Disable ACLs in favor of bucket policies

topics.name: EBS (Elastic Block Store)
topics.explanation: Persistent block storage volumes for EC2 instances. Like attaching a hard drive to a server. Data persists independently of EC2 instance lifecycle.

volume_types.use_case: Default — OS volumes, boot volumes, most workloads
volume_types.advantage: Baseline 3,000 IOPS free regardless of volume size

volume_types.use_case: Legacy — migrate to gp3 for better performance and lower cost
Mission-critical databases (Oracle, SQL Server), sub-millisecond latency
Big data, log processing, data warehouses — sequential large reads
Infrequently accessed data, lowest cost block storage

key_features: Single AZ — cannot be attached to instances in different AZ
Can be attached to only one EC2 instance at a time (except io1/io2 Multi-Attach)
Snapshots backed to S3 — durable, can be used for cross-region copies
Encryption via KMS

topics.name: EFS (Elastic File System)
topics.explanation: Fully managed, serverless, elastic NFS file system. Automatically grows and shrinks. Can be mounted by thousands of EC2 instances simultaneously across multiple AZs.
key_features: Multi-AZ — data replicated across multiple AZs
Concurrent access from thousands of instances
Automatic scaling — no pre-provisioning
NFS v4 protocol
Linux only (no Windows support)
use_cases: Shared file storage for containerized apps

vs_ebs.efs: Multi-instance access, auto-scaling, multi-AZ, NFS, Linux only
vs_ebs.ebs: Single instance, fixed size, single AZ, block storage, Linux and Windows

topics.name: RDS (Relational Database Service)
topics.explanation: Fully managed relational database service. AWS handles provisioning, patching, backups, and failover. Supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Db2.
key_features: Automated backups (up to 35 days retention)
Point-in-time recovery
Multi-AZ for high availability
Read Replicas for read scaling
Automated OS and DB engine patching
Encryption at rest (KMS) and in transit (SSL/TLS)
Enhanced Monitoring and Performance Insights
topics.create_rds_cli:

topics.connect_python:
import psycopg2

conn = psycopg2.connect(
    host='prod-postgres.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com',
    port=5432,
    database='analytics',
    user='admin',
    password='SecurePass123!',
    sslmode='require'  # Always use SSL
)
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders')
print(cursor.fetchone())
drawbacks: Vertical scaling requires downtime (changing instance class)
Storage can only grow, not shrink
Limited to 64TB storage for most engines
solutions: Use Aurora for automatic storage scaling and better performance
Use Read Replicas for read-heavy workloads
Use RDS Proxy for connection pooling

topics.name: RDS Multi-AZ & Read Replicas
topics.explanation: Two key features for RDS availability and performance scaling.

multi_az.description: Synchronous replication to a standby instance in a different AZ. Automatic failover on primary failure (60-120 seconds). Used for HIGH AVAILABILITY, not performance.
how_it_works: Primary instance handles all reads and writes
Changes synchronously replicated to standby before acknowledging write
On primary failure: DNS updates to point to standby (60-120s)
Applications reconnect to same endpoint — no code change needed
multi_az.note: Standby is NOT accessible for reads — it only serves as failover target

read_replicas.description: Asynchronous replication to up to 15 read replicas. Used for READ SCALING and offloading read traffic from primary.
key_points: Separate endpoint — application must route read queries explicitly
Can be in same AZ, different AZ, or different Region
Can be promoted to standalone DB (breaks replication)
Supported engines: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server
use_cases: Cross-region disaster recovery (promote replica in another region)

topics.name: Aurora (MySQL & PostgreSQL Compatible)
topics.explanation: AWS proprietary database engine that is MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible but delivers up to 5x MySQL and 3x PostgreSQL performance. Decoupled storage and compute architecture.

architecture.storage: Distributed, self-healing storage across 6 copies in 3 AZs. Auto-scales from 10GB to 128TB. No need to provision storage upfront.
architecture.compute: Primary instance (read/write) + up to 15 Aurora Replicas (read-only). All share the same storage volume.

key_features: 6 copies of data across 3 AZs (2 per AZ)
Self-healing — automatically detects and repairs storage corruption
Cluster endpoint (write) + reader endpoint (read) + individual endpoints
Backtrack: rewind DB to any point in last 72 hours WITHOUT restoring snapshot
Global Database: cross-region replication with < 1 second lag
Aurora Serverless v2: auto-scales compute in fine-grained increments

aurora_serverless_v2.description: Aurora compute that scales instantly based on workload. Measured in ACUs (Aurora Capacity Units). Min: 0.5 ACU, Max: 128 ACU.
aurora_serverless_v2.use_case: Variable/unpredictable workloads, dev/test environments, multi-tenant SaaS
aurora_serverless_v2.benefit: Pay only for capacity used. Scale from 0 to full capacity in seconds.
aurora_serverless_v2.example: Min 0.5 ACU ($0.06/hour idle) → spike to 64 ACU ($7.68/hour at peak) → back to 0.5 ACU automatically

drawbacks: More expensive than standard RDS (20-30% premium)
Aurora-specific features create some lock-in
solutions: Cost is offset by better performance (fewer/smaller instances needed)
MySQL/PostgreSQL compatibility minimizes lock-in for application code

topics.name: DynamoDB
topics.explanation: Fully managed, serverless NoSQL key-value and document database. Single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. Automatically replicates across 3 AZs.

core_concepts.description: Collection of items. No fixed schema — each item can have different attributes.
Records (like rows). Each item identified by primary key.
Data fields within an item (like columns, but flexible).
Partition Key (simple) or Partition Key + Sort Key (composite). Must be unique per item.
core_concepts.example: Partition Key: customer_id. Sort Key: order_date. Together uniquely identify each order.

options.description: Pre-specify Read Capacity Units (RCU) and Write Capacity Units (WCU). Use Auto Scaling to adjust.
options.best_for: Predictable, steady traffic. Cheaper for consistent workloads.

options.description: Pay per request. No capacity planning. Instantly scales to any traffic level.
options.best_for: Unpredictable traffic, new applications, spiky workloads.

example.boto3_operations:
import boto3
from boto3.dynamodb.conditions import Key, Attr

dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb', region_name='us-east-1')
table = dynamodb.Table('Orders')

# Put item
table.put_item(Item={
    'customer_id': 'CUST-123',
    'order_id': 'ORD-456',
    'order_date': '2024-01-15',
    'amount': 99.99,
    'status': 'shipped',
    'items': ['Widget A', 'Gadget B']
})

# Get item
response = table.get_item(
    Key={'customer_id': 'CUST-123', 'order_id': 'ORD-456'}
)
item = response['Item']

# Query (efficient — uses index)
response = table.query(
    KeyConditionExpression=Key('customer_id').eq('CUST-123') &
                            Key('order_date').between('2024-01-01', '2024-01-31')
)

# Scan (full table — avoid for large tables)
response = table.scan(
    FilterExpression=Attr('status').eq('pending')
)

global_secondary_index.description: Secondary index with different partition key and optional sort key. Allows efficient querying on non-primary-key attributes.
global_secondary_index.example:
Table: Orders (PK: customer_id, SK: order_id)
GSI: StatusIndex (PK: status, SK: order_date)
Query all 'pending' orders sorted by date — efficient with GSI

drawbacks: No SQL — no joins, no complex aggregations
Item size limit: 400KB
Query patterns must be designed upfront (access pattern driven design)
Scans are expensive for large tables
solutions: Design data model around access patterns first
Use GSIs for alternative query patterns
Use DynamoDB Streams + Lambda for aggregations
Use single-table design pattern to model relationships

topics.name: Redshift
topics.explanation: AWS fully managed petabyte-scale data warehouse built on PostgreSQL. Columnar storage, MPP (Massively Parallel Processing) architecture. Optimized for OLAP analytical queries on large datasets.

architecture.leader_node: Receives SQL queries from clients, parses, creates execution plans, coordinates compute nodes, aggregates results.
architecture.compute_nodes: Each node processes its slice of data in parallel. Communicate via high-bandwidth interconnect.
architecture.node_slices: Each compute node is divided into slices. A slice processes a portion of the node's data in parallel.
architecture.storage: Columnar: stores each column separately → reads only queried columns. 3x-10x compression vs row storage.

key_features: Columnar storage + compression (ZSTD, LZO, AZ64)
Massively Parallel Processing (MPP)
Redshift Spectrum: query S3 data lake directly
Concurrency Scaling: burst capacity for spiky workloads
Result caching: repeated queries return cached results instantly
AQUA (Advanced Query Accelerator): in-memory cache layer
Zero-ETL integration with Aurora for near-real-time replication

distribution_styles.description: Redshift automatically chooses best distribution. Default.
Rows distributed evenly across nodes using round-robin.
distribution_styles.use_when: No clear join key, small dimension tables

distribution_styles.description: Rows with same distribution key land on same node. Eliminates network shuffle for joins on that key.
distribution_styles.use_when: Fact table joining to large dimension tables on the same key

distribution_styles.description: Entire table copied to every node. No joins needed.
distribution_styles.use_when: Small dimension tables (< 1M rows) joined frequently

sort_keys.description: Data stored in sorted order on disk. Zone maps (min/max per block) enable block pruning for range queries.
sort_keys.compound_sort_key: Primary sort, then secondary. Best for filters on leading columns.
sort_keys.interleaved_sort_key: Equal weight to all key columns. Better for multi-column filters.

topics.name: ElastiCache (Redis & Memcached)
topics.explanation: Fully managed in-memory caching service. Sub-millisecond latency. Reduces database load for frequently accessed data.

redis_vs_memcached.redis: Strings, Lists, Sets, Sorted Sets, Hashes, Streams

topics.redis_example:
import redis

r = redis.Redis(
    host='my-cluster.cache.amazonaws.com',
    port=6379,
    ssl=True
)

# Cache-aside pattern
def get_user(user_id: str):
    cached = r.get(f'user:{user_id}')
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)

    # Cache miss — fetch from DB
    user = db.query(f'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}')
    r.setex(f'user:{user_id}', 3600, json.dumps(user))  # TTL: 1 hour
    return user

# Sorted set for leaderboard
r.zadd('game:leaderboard', {'alice': 5000, 'bob': 3200, 'carol': 4800})
top_players = r.zrevrange('game:leaderboard', 0, 9, withscores=True)

# Pub/Sub for real-time events
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.subscribe('order-events')
r.publish('order-events', json.dumps({'order_id': 123, 'status': 'shipped'}))
drawbacks: Data loss risk on Redis node failure if persistence not configured
Memory-only storage — limited by RAM size
Cache invalidation complexity
solutions: Enable Redis persistence (RDB + AOF) for durability
Use Multi-AZ with auto-failover
Implement cache invalidation strategy (TTL, event-driven invalidation)

topics.name: DynamoDB Streams
topics.explanation: Captures item-level changes in DynamoDB tables as an ordered stream of records. Records available for 24 hours. Used to trigger downstream processing on data changes.

stream_record_types.description: Only key attributes of modified item

use_cases: Trigger Lambda on DynamoDB changes (CDC pattern)
Replicate data to ElasticSearch for search

topics.name: VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
topics.explanation: Logically isolated virtual network within AWS. You control IP ranges, subnets, routing, internet access, and network security. Every AWS account gets a default VPC per region.
key_components: CIDR block: IP range for the VPC (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16 = 65,536 IPs)
Subnets: subdivisions of VPC CIDR in specific AZs
Route Tables: rules for directing traffic
NAT Gateway: enables private subnet instances to access internet without being publicly accessible
Security Groups: stateful virtual firewall at instance level
Network ACLs: stateless firewall at subnet level

topics.name: Security Groups & NACLs
topics.explanation: Two layers of network security in a VPC. Security Groups are stateful (track connections). NACLs are stateless (must define both inbound and outbound rules).

security_groups.description: Virtual firewall at the instance/ENI level. Stateful: if inbound traffic is allowed, return traffic is automatically allowed.
security_groups.rules: Inbound and outbound rules. Deny all inbound by default. Allow all outbound by default.
security_groups.example:
# Allow HTTPS from internet + PostgreSQL from app servers only:
Inbound Rules:
  Port 443 (HTTPS) from 0.0.0.0/0 (internet)
  Port 5432 (PostgreSQL) from sg-appserver (app security group)

Outbound Rules:
  All traffic (default)

nacls.description: Firewall at the subnet level. Stateless: must define both inbound AND outbound rules explicitly. Rules evaluated in order (lowest number first).
nacls.use_case: Block specific IP ranges at subnet level. Additional defense layer.
nacls.example:
Rule 100: ALLOW TCP 443 from 0.0.0.0/0  (HTTPS inbound)
Rule 200: ALLOW TCP 1024-65535 from 0.0.0.0/0  (ephemeral ports for responses)
Rule 300: DENY  TCP 0.0.0.0/0 from 192.168.1.0/24  (block specific CIDR)
Rule *:   DENY  ALL

topics.name: VPC Endpoints (Gateway & Interface)
topics.explanation: VPC Endpoints allow private connectivity between your VPC and AWS services without going over the public internet. No internet gateway, NAT device, or VPN connection required.

endpoint_types.description: Free. For S3 and DynamoDB only. Route table entry added — traffic routed through AWS internal network.
endpoint_types.setup: Add route to route table: destination=pl-xxxxx (S3 prefix list) → target=vpce-xxxxx
endpoint_types.benefit: Spark on EMR accessing S3 stays on AWS internal network. No NAT Gateway costs for S3 traffic (saves $0.045/GB).

endpoint_types.description: Creates ENI (Elastic Network Interface) in your subnet with private IP. Supports 170+ AWS services.
endpoint_types.use_case: Private connectivity to AWS services from private subnets without internet

topics.name: Route 53 (DNS)
topics.explanation: AWS highly available and scalable DNS (Domain Name System) service. Also provides domain registration, health checks, and traffic routing.

routing_policies.description: Single record pointing to one resource. No health checks.
routing_policies.use_case: Single web server, static S3 website

routing_policies.description: Route traffic by percentage weights to multiple resources.
routing_policies.use_case: A/B testing (10% to new version, 90% to old), gradual migration

routing_policies.description: Route to resource with lowest latency for the user.
routing_policies.use_case: Multi-region apps — users in Europe go to EU region, US users to US region

routing_policies.description: Primary-secondary setup. Health checks route to secondary if primary fails.
Route based on user's geographic location (country, continent).
Returns multiple IP addresses. Client-side load balancing. Health checks on each record.
routing_policies.use_case: Simple load balancing when ELB is overkill

routing_policies.description: Route based on client IP CIDR.
routing_policies.use_case: Route corporate office traffic differently from public internet traffic

topics.name: CloudFront (CDN)
topics.explanation: Global Content Delivery Network (CDN) with 400+ edge locations worldwide. Caches content at edge locations closest to users. Reduces latency and origin server load.
how_it_works: User requests content → DNS resolves to nearest CloudFront edge
Edge checks cache — if HIT: returns cached content (low latency)
If MISS: edge fetches from origin (S3, ALB, EC2, API Gateway, custom)
Edge caches response → serves subsequent requests from cache
key_features: HTTPS termination at edge
Custom SSL certificates via ACM
Geo-restriction (block/allow by country)
Lambda@Edge: run code at edge locations
CloudFront Functions: lightweight JS at edge (< 1ms execution)
Signed URLs and Signed Cookies for private content
Field-level encryption for sensitive data
Real-time logs

Origin: my-bucket.s3.amazonaws.com
Behaviors:
  - Path: /api/*  → ALB origin (dynamic)
  - Path: /*      → S3 origin (static files)
HTTPS: Redirect HTTP to HTTPS

topics.name: Transit Gateway
topics.explanation: Hub-and-spoke network transit hub that interconnects VPCs and on-premises networks. Replaces complex VPC peering mesh with a central hub.
topics.without_tgw: With 5 VPCs needing full mesh: 10 VPC peering connections needed. With 10 VPCs: 45 connections.
topics.with_tgw: All VPCs connect to Transit Gateway. N VPCs = N connections total.
use_cases: Centralized network inspection (route all traffic through security VPC)
Hybrid connectivity (connect on-premises to all VPCs via single gateway)
Shared services VPC (DNS, Active Directory accessible from all VPCs)

topics.name: AWS Glue
topics.explanation: Fully managed serverless ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service. Automatically discovers data schemas via crawlers. Runs Apache Spark and Python Shell jobs without managing infrastructure.

components.description: Central metadata repository. Stores table definitions, schemas, and location of data. Compatible with Apache Hive Metastore. Used by Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR.
Automatically scan data sources (S3, RDS, DynamoDB, JDBC) and populate Data Catalog with schemas and partitions.
Serverless Spark (Python/Scala) or Python Shell jobs. Auto-scales workers. Billed per DPU-hour.
Visual ETL job builder. Drag-and-drop interface generates Glue Spark code.
Visual data preparation tool for analysts. 250+ pre-built transformations. No code needed.

glue_job_example.pyspark_etl:
import sys
from awsglue.transforms import *
from awsglue.utils import getResolvedOptions
from pyspark.context import SparkContext
from awsglue.context import GlueContext
from awsglue.job import Job
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, to_date, when, lit

args = getResolvedOptions(sys.argv, ['JOB_NAME', 'execution_date'])
sc = SparkContext()
glueContext = GlueContext(sc)
spark = glueContext.spark_session
job = Job(glueContext)
job.init(args['JOB_NAME'], args)

# Read from Data Catalog (S3 source)
raw_orders = glueContext.create_dynamic_frame.from_catalog(
    database='analytics_raw',
    table_name='orders',
    push_down_predicate=f"partition_date = '{args['execution_date']}'"
)

# Convert to Spark DataFrame and transform
df = raw_orders.toDF()
df_clean = (df
    .filter(col('order_id').isNotNull())
    .withColumn('order_date', to_date(col('order_date_str'), 'yyyy-MM-dd'))
    .withColumn('amount', col('amount_str').cast('decimal(18,2)'))
    .withColumn('status', when(col('status') == 'COMP', 'completed').otherwise(col('status')))
    .drop('order_date_str', 'amount_str')
    .dropDuplicates(['order_id'])
)

# Write to S3 as Parquet
df_clean.write \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('order_date') \
    .parquet(f"s3://my-data-lake/silver/orders/")

job.commit()

  --schedule 'cron(0 1 * * ? *)'

# Start crawler
aws glue start-crawler --name raw-orders-crawler
drawbacks: Cold start latency (1-3 minutes for Glue job startup)
DPU pricing can be expensive for small jobs
Limited Spark version compared to EMR
solutions: Use Glue job bookmarks to process only new data
Use Python Shell jobs for lightweight tasks (cheaper than Spark)
Use EMR for complex Spark workloads needing latest Spark versions

topics.name: Glue Data Catalog
topics.explanation: The Glue Data Catalog is the metadata repository that acts as a central catalog for all your data assets. It is Hive Metastore compatible — Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR all use it.

structure.database: Logical grouping of tables (like a schema)
structure.table: Metadata about a dataset — schema, location, format, partition keys

topics.name: Amazon EMR
topics.explanation: Elastic MapReduce — managed big data platform running Apache Spark, Hadoop, Hive, Presto, HBase, and more on EC2 or Serverless. Decouples storage (S3) from compute (EC2 cluster).

cluster_types.description: Launch EC2 cluster with Master, Core, and Task nodes. Full control over configuration.

node_types.description: Coordinates cluster, runs YARN ResourceManager, HDFS NameNode. 1 per cluster.
Run tasks AND store data in HDFS. At least 1 required.
Run tasks only (no HDFS storage). Spot instances — can be removed without data loss.

cluster_types.description: No cluster management. Submit Spark/Hive jobs and AWS handles all infrastructure. Auto-scales workers per job.
cluster_types.benefit: No idle cluster costs. Pay only during job execution.
cluster_types.use_case: Intermittent batch jobs, data engineering teams wanting simplicity

cluster_types.description: Run Spark jobs on existing EKS cluster. Leverage Kubernetes for resource sharing.
cluster_types.use_case: Teams with EKS expertise, multi-tenant Spark on K8s

strategies: Use Spot Instances for Task nodes (up to 90% savings). Master+Core On-Demand for reliability.
Use EMR Serverless for intermittent workloads — no idle cluster cost
Store data in S3 (not HDFS) — terminate cluster when not processing
Use EMR Auto Scaling to add/remove Task nodes based on workload
Use instance fleets to bid on multiple Spot instance types

topics.name: Amazon Athena
topics.explanation: Serverless interactive query service that runs SQL directly on data in S3. No infrastructure to manage. Pay per query based on data scanned. Uses Presto/Trino engine.
key_features: Standard SQL (ANSI SQL compatible)
Supports Parquet, ORC, JSON, CSV, Avro, TSV
Uses Glue Data Catalog for schema definitions
Query results cached for reuse
Federated queries: query RDS, DynamoDB, ElasticSearch via Lambda connectors
Athena for Apache Spark: run Spark notebooks serverlessly
topics.pricing: Pay per query: $5 per TB scanned. FREE if query returns 0 bytes (e.g., result cache hit).
cost_optimization: Use Parquet/ORC (columnar) — 10x less data scanned vs CSV
Partition data by date/region — partition pruning reduces scanned data
Use compression (SNAPPY, ZSTD) — less data scanned
Use columnar format + partitioning together for 99% cost reduction vs raw CSV

example.query_s3_data:
-- Query S3 Parquet data via Glue Catalog
SELECT
    region,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
    COUNT(*) AS orders,
    SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM analytics_silver.orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN DATE '2024-01-01' AND DATE '2024-12-31'
  AND status = 'completed'
GROUP BY 1, 2
ORDER BY month, revenue DESC;
example.boto3_query:
import boto3, time

athena = boto3.client('athena', region_name='us-east-1')

# Start query
response = athena.start_query_execution(
    QueryString='SELECT COUNT(*) FROM analytics_silver.orders WHERE order_date = \'2024-01-15\'',
    QueryExecutionContext={'Database': 'analytics_silver'},
    ResultConfiguration={'OutputLocation': 's3://my-athena-results/'}
)
query_id = response['QueryExecutionId']

# Wait for completion
while True:
    status = athena.get_query_execution(QueryExecutionId=query_id)['QueryExecution']['Status']['State']
    if status in ['SUCCEEDED', 'FAILED', 'CANCELLED']:
        break
    time.sleep(1)

# Get results
results = athena.get_query_results(QueryExecutionId=query_id)
for row in results['ResultSet']['Rows']:
    print([col['VarCharValue'] for col in row['Data']])

drawbacks: No indexes — always scans data (mitigated by partitioning + columnar format)
Query timeout: 30 minutes maximum
Not suitable for OLTP or high-concurrency workloads
Results not cached between sessions by default

topics.name: AWS Lake Formation
topics.explanation: Service to build, secure, and manage a data lake on S3 with centralized fine-grained access control. Sits on top of Glue Data Catalog and S3, adding column-level and row-level security.
key_capabilities: Fine-grained access control: column-level, row-level, cell-level security
Data lake blueprints: automated data ingestion from databases
Cross-account data sharing via RAM (Resource Access Manager)
Integration with Athena, Redshift Spectrum, Glue, EMR

topics.name: Kinesis Data Streams
topics.explanation: Real-time data streaming service. Ingest and process streaming data at scale. Data retained for 24 hours (default) to 365 days. Multiple consumers can read same stream independently.

core_concepts.description: Unit of capacity. Each shard: 1MB/s ingest, 2MB/s read, 1000 records/s. Scale by adding shards.
Data unit. Max 1MB per record. Contains: data blob, partition key, sequence number.
Determines which shard a record goes to (via MD5 hash). Same partition key = same shard = ordered.
Unique identifier assigned by Kinesis. Records in a shard are ordered by sequence number.

types.throughput: 2MB/s shared across all consumers per shard

consumers.lambda_trigger: Kinesis → Lambda is a built-in trigger. Lambda polls shards and invokes function with batches of records.

drawbacks: Manual shard management — must pre-provision capacity
Shard hot spots if partition key distribution is uneven
Shard splitting/merging causes brief processing pause
solutions: Use Kinesis Data Streams On-Demand mode for automatic scaling
Choose high-cardinality partition keys for even distribution
Monitor GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds — high value means consumer lag

topics.name: Kinesis Data Firehose
topics.explanation: Fully managed delivery stream that loads streaming data into destinations (S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, HTTP endpoints, Splunk). Automatic scaling, built-in transformation via Lambda, no consumers to manage.
key_features: Fully managed — no shards, no consumer code
Built-in buffering: buffer by size (1-128MB) or time (60-900s)
Lambda transformation: transform records before delivery
Format conversion: JSON to Parquet/ORC automatically
Data compression: GZIP, SNAPPY, ZIP, HADOOP_SNAPPY
S3 delivery with automatic date-based prefix partitioning

topics.name: Amazon MSK (Managed Kafka)
topics.explanation: Fully managed Apache Kafka service on AWS. AWS handles broker provisioning, configuration, patching, and availability. You use standard Kafka APIs — no code changes.
key_features: Compatible with Apache Kafka (standard client libraries work as-is)
Multi-AZ broker deployment (3 AZs)
Integrated with IAM, VPC, KMS, CloudWatch
MSK Connect: managed Kafka Connect
MSK Serverless: auto-scales without capacity planning
topics.producer_example:
from kafka import KafkaProducer
import json

producer = KafkaProducer(
    bootstrap_servers=['b-1.my-msk.kafka.us-east-1.amazonaws.com:9092'],
    value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode('utf-8'),

)

for order in orders:

topics.name: Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service)
topics.explanation: Fully managed message queuing service. Decouples producers and consumers. Messages persist until explicitly deleted by consumer. Each message consumed by ONE consumer.

queue_types.use_case: High-throughput workloads where ordering and duplicates can be handled
Financial transactions, order processing where order matters

key_concepts.description: Period a message is invisible after being received by a consumer. If consumer doesn't delete within timeout, message becomes visible again for retry. Default: 30 seconds.
Queue for messages that fail processing N times. Isolates bad messages for analysis.
Wait up to 20 seconds for messages instead of returning empty if queue is empty. Reduces API calls and cost.

topics.example:
import boto3, json
sqs = boto3.client('sqs', region_name='us-east-1')
QUEUE_URL = 'https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/order-processing'

# Send message
sqs.send_message(
    QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
    MessageBody=json.dumps({'order_id': 123, 'amount': 99.99}),
    DelaySeconds=0
)

# Receive and process messages
while True:
    response = sqs.receive_message(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        MaxNumberOfMessages=10,
        WaitTimeSeconds=20,      # Long polling
        VisibilityTimeout=60
    )
    for msg in response.get('Messages', []):
        body = json.loads(msg['Body'])
        process_order(body)
        # Delete after successful processing
        sqs.delete_message(
            QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
            ReceiptHandle=msg['ReceiptHandle']
        )

# Flink SQL on Managed Flink:
# Read from Kinesis, aggregate, write to S3

CREATE TABLE kinesis_orders (
  order_id STRING,
  amount DOUBLE,
  region STRING,
  event_time TIMESTAMP(3),
  WATERMARK FOR event_time AS event_time - INTERVAL '5' SECOND
) WITH (
  'connector' = 'kinesis',
  'stream' = 'order-events',
  'aws.region' = 'us-east-1',
  'scan.stream.initpos' = 'LATEST',
  'format' = 'json'
);

CREATE TABLE s3_revenue_sink (
  window_start TIMESTAMP,
  region STRING,
  total_revenue DOUBLE
) WITH (
  'connector' = 'filesystem',
  'path' = 's3://my-bucket/real-time-revenue/',
  'format' = 'parquet'
);

INSERT INTO s3_revenue_sink
SELECT
  TUMBLE_START(event_time, INTERVAL '5' MINUTE) as window_start,
  region,
  SUM(amount) as total_revenue
FROM kinesis_orders
GROUP BY TUMBLE(event_time, INTERVAL '5' MINUTE), region;

topics.example:
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: Data Pipeline Infrastructure

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, staging, prod]

Resources:
  DataLakeBucket:
    Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
    Properties:
      BucketName: !Sub 'data-lake-${Environment}-${AWS::AccountId}'
      VersioningConfiguration:
        Status: Enabled
      BucketEncryption:
        ServerSideEncryptionConfiguration:
          - ServerSideEncryptionByDefault:
              SSEAlgorithm: aws:kms
      LifecycleConfiguration:
        Rules:
          - Id: MoveToIA
            Status: Enabled
            Transitions:
              - Days: 30
                StorageClass: STANDARD_IA

  GlueJobRole:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              Service: glue.amazonaws.com
            Action: sts:AssumeRole
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSGlueServiceRole
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: S3Access
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action: ['s3:GetObject', 's3:PutObject']
                Resource: !Sub '${DataLakeBucket.Arn}/*'

Outputs:
  BucketName:
    Value: !Ref DataLakeBucket
    Export:
      Name: !Sub '${AWS::StackName}-DataLakeBucket'

topics.name: CDK (Cloud Development Kit)
topics.explanation: Define AWS infrastructure using familiar programming languages (Python, TypeScript, Java, C#, Go). CDK synthesizes CloudFormation templates. Higher-level abstractions (Constructs) simplify common patterns.
topics.example_python:
from aws_cdk import (
    Stack, App,
    aws_s3 as s3,
    aws_glue as glue,
    aws_iam as iam,
    Duration, RemovalPolicy
)
from constructs import Construct

class DataLakeStack(Stack):
    def __init__(self, scope: Construct, id: str, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(scope, id, **kwargs)

        # S3 Data Lake bucket
        bucket = s3.Bucket(self, 'DataLakeBucket',
            bucket_name=f'data-lake-{self.account}',
            versioned=True,
            encryption=s3.BucketEncryption.KMS_MANAGED,
            removal_policy=RemovalPolicy.RETAIN,
            lifecycle_rules=[
                s3.LifecycleRule(
                    transitions=[s3.Transition(
                        storage_class=s3.StorageClass.INFREQUENT_ACCESS,
                        transition_after=Duration.days(30)
                    )]
                )
            ]
        )

        # Glue job IAM role
        glue_role = iam.Role(self, 'GlueRole',
            assumed_by=iam.ServicePrincipal('glue.amazonaws.com'),
            managed_policies=[iam.ManagedPolicy.from_aws_managed_policy_name('service-role/AWSGlueServiceRole')]
        )
        bucket.grant_read_write(glue_role)

app = App()
DataLakeStack(app, 'DataLakeStack')
app.synth()

# Find top Lambda errors in last 24 hours:
fields @timestamp, @message
| filter @message like /ERROR/
| stats count(*) as error_count by bin(1h)
| sort error_count desc
| limit 20

# Lambda duration percentiles:
filter @type = "REPORT"
| stats avg(@duration), max(@duration),
        percentile(@duration, 95) as p95,
        percentile(@duration, 99) as p99
  by bin(5m)

fields @timestamp, @message
| filter MetricName = 'GetRecords.IteratorAgeMilliseconds'
| stats avg(Average) by bin(5m)

topics.python_example:
from aws_xray_sdk.core import xray_recorder, patch_all
import boto3

# Auto-instrument all AWS SDK calls
patch_all()

@xray_recorder.capture('process_order')
def process_order(order_id: str):
    with xray_recorder.in_subsegment('fetch_from_dynamodb') as subsegment:
        subsegment.put_annotation('order_id', order_id)
        dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
        table = dynamodb.Table('Orders')
        response = table.get_item(Key={'order_id': order_id})

    with xray_recorder.in_subsegment('write_to_s3'):
        s3 = boto3.client('s3')
        s3.put_object(Bucket='processed', Key=f'{order_id}.json', Body=str(response))

topics.example:
import boto3
kms = boto3.client('kms')

# Create CMK
key = kms.create_key(
    Description='Data Lake Encryption Key',
    KeyUsage='ENCRYPT_DECRYPT',
    Policy=json.dumps({
        'Version': '2012-10-17',
        'Statement': [{
            'Sid': 'Allow data engineers',
            'Effect': 'Allow',
            'Principal': {'AWS': 'arn:aws:iam::123:role/DataEngineerRole'},
            'Action': ['kms:Encrypt', 'kms:Decrypt', 'kms:GenerateDataKey'],
            'Resource': '*'
        }]
    })
)

# Encrypt data
encrypted = kms.encrypt(
    KeyId=key['KeyMetadata']['KeyId'],
    Plaintext=b'sensitive data'
)

# Decrypt
decrypted = kms.decrypt(CiphertextBlob=encrypted['CiphertextBlob'])

topics.name: Spot Instances Strategy
topics.explanation: Spot Instances can be interrupted by AWS with 2-minute notice when EC2 needs the capacity. Use strategies to minimize interruption impact.

strategies.description: Use Spot Fleets or EC2 Fleet with multiple instance types and AZs. If one pool is interrupted, others continue.
strategies.example: Fleet: m5.xlarge, m5a.xlarge, m4.xlarge, m5.2xlarge (all in pool)

strategies.description: Detect 2-minute warning via EC2 metadata or EventBridge. Checkpoint work, drain gracefully.
strategies.code:
import requests
# Poll instance metadata every 5 seconds:
response = requests.get(
    'http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/spot/instance-action',
    timeout=1
)
if response.status_code == 200:  # Interruption notice received
    action = response.json()  # {'action': 'terminate', 'time': '...'}
    checkpoint_current_work()
    graceful_shutdown()

strategies.description: Regularly save processing state to S3/DynamoDB. On interruption/restart, resume from last checkpoint.

topics.name: Multi-Account Data Platform Architecture
topics.explanation: Enterprise data platforms use multiple AWS accounts for isolation, security, and cost allocation.

account_structure.purpose: AWS Organizations root, SCPs, consolidated billing only
Development data lake, Glue jobs, ETL development
Production data lake, live pipelines, secure data
Redshift, QuickSight, Athena for business users
Shared: Glue Catalog, Lake Formation, Airflow (MWAA), monitoring

topics.cross_account_data_access:
