questions.question: What is the difference between S3 storage classes and when would you use each?
questions.answer: S3 Standard: frequently accessed data, no retrieval fee, highest cost ($0.023/GB). Standard-IA: infrequently accessed but needs fast retrieval ($0.0125/GB + retrieval fee). Glacier Instant Retrieval: quarterly access, millisecond retrieval ($0.004/GB). Glacier Flexible: archival, hours retrieval ($0.0036/GB). Glacier Deep Archive: 7-10 year compliance, 12-48hr retrieval ($0.00099/GB). S3 Intelligent-Tiering: unknown patterns, auto-moves between tiers. For a data lake: Standard for last 30 days, Standard-IA for 30-90 days, Glacier for 90+ days via lifecycle policy.
questions.drawbacks: Minimum storage duration fees apply: Standard-IA (30 days), Glacier IR (90 days), Glacier Flexible (90 days), Deep Archive (180 days). Retrieving prematurely incurs charges.
questions.solutions: Model access patterns before choosing class. Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for uncertain patterns. Set lifecycle policies to automate transitions.

questions.question: Explain the difference between S3 and EBS and EFS.
questions.answer: S3: Object storage. Accessed via HTTP API. Unlimited storage. 11 nines durability. Global namespace. Best for data lakes, backups, static files, ML datasets. EBS: Block storage. Attached to ONE EC2 instance at a time. Single AZ. Must pre-provision size. Like a hard drive — best for OS volumes, databases, applications needing file system. EFS: Network File System. Mounted by THOUSANDS of EC2 instances simultaneously. Multi-AZ. Auto-scales. Linux only. Best for shared content, containers sharing data, ML training data.
questions.drawbacks: S3 has higher latency than EBS/EFS for frequent small reads. EBS is AZ-locked. EFS is more expensive than EBS per GB.
questions.solutions: Use S3 for batch analytics workloads. Use EBS for databases. Use EFS for shared workloads across instances or containers.

questions.question: What is IAM Role vs IAM User and when should you use each?
questions.answer: IAM User: permanent identity with long-term credentials (password, access keys). For humans needing console access or programmatic access from outside AWS. IAM Role: temporary identity with short-term credentials via STS. Assumed by AWS services (EC2, Lambda, Glue), applications, or cross-account entities. No permanent credentials — automatically rotated. Best practice: use roles for everything in AWS (EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles, ECS task roles). Use users only for human console access with MFA.
questions.drawbacks: Long-term access keys for IAM users are a security risk if leaked. They don't expire automatically.
questions.solutions: Enforce MFA for all IAM users. Rotate access keys every 90 days. Use Secrets Manager or IAM Identity Center for programmatic access. Use roles for all AWS services.

questions.question: What is the difference between Security Groups and NACLs?
questions.answer: Security Groups: instance-level virtual firewall. Stateful — return traffic automatically allowed. Allow rules only. All rules evaluated. NACLs: subnet-level firewall. Stateless — must define inbound AND outbound rules explicitly. Allow and Deny rules. Rules evaluated in order (first match wins). Security Groups are the primary tool — applied to every instance. NACLs are additional defense — useful to block specific IP ranges at subnet level.
questions.drawbacks: Forgetting NACLs are stateless causes outbound rule issues — blocked response traffic even when inbound is allowed.
questions.solutions: Always allow ephemeral ports (1024-65535) in NACL outbound rules for response traffic. Use Security Groups as primary control, NACLs for bulk IP blocking.

questions.question: How does VPC Endpoint differ from NAT Gateway for accessing S3?
questions.answer: NAT Gateway: allows private subnet instances to access the internet (including S3 via public internet). Costs $0.045/GB data processed + hourly charge. Traffic goes over public internet. VPC Gateway Endpoint (S3 and DynamoDB only): free. Routes traffic from private subnet directly to S3 via AWS internal network — never touches the internet. Faster, more secure, eliminates NAT Gateway charges for S3 traffic. A data lake on S3 with Spark on EMR in private subnets should ALWAYS use S3 Gateway Endpoint.
questions.drawbacks: S3 Gateway Endpoint only works within the same region. Cross-region S3 access still requires internet via NAT.
questions.solutions: Create S3 VPC Gateway Endpoint and update route tables. For cross-region access, use PrivateLink via Interface Endpoint in both regions.

questions.question: What is the difference between RDS Multi-AZ and Read Replicas?
questions.answer: Multi-AZ: synchronous replication to standby in different AZ. Standby is NOT readable. Automatic failover in 60-120 seconds. Purpose: HIGH AVAILABILITY. Read Replicas: asynchronous replication to up to 15 replicas. All replicas are readable (separate endpoints). No automatic failover — must be promoted manually. Purpose: READ SCALING and reporting offload. Can also be cross-region. Multi-AZ = disaster recovery. Read Replica = performance.
questions.drawbacks: Multi-AZ doubles DB cost with no performance benefit. Read Replicas have replication lag (usually < 1 second) — stale reads possible.
questions.solutions: Use Multi-AZ in production for HA. Use Read Replicas for BI/analytics queries to avoid impacting primary. Route reporting tools to replica endpoint.

questions.question: Explain how AWS Glue Data Catalog works and why it's important.
questions.answer: Glue Data Catalog is a central metadata repository storing table schemas, locations, partition information, and data formats. It's Hive Metastore compatible — multiple services (Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, Glue ETL) share the same metadata. Crawlers automatically populate the catalog by scanning S3, RDS, or JDBC sources. This enables schema-on-read: data is stored in S3 as files, and the catalog defines how to interpret it. Critical because without it, every query tool needs its own schema definition.
questions.drawbacks: Crawlers can infer wrong schema (all strings) for poorly formatted data. Partitions must be manually added or refreshed after new data loads.
questions.solutions: Define schemas manually via Glue for production tables instead of relying on crawlers. Use MSCK REPAIR TABLE in Athena or add partitions via Glue API after each Spark job.

questions.question: What is the difference between Kinesis Data Streams and Kinesis Data Firehose?
questions.answer: Kinesis Data Streams: raw streaming platform — you manage consumers (Lambda, Flink, custom apps), control processing, data retained 24hr-365days, multiple independent consumers possible, real-time (70-200ms). Kinesis Firehose: managed delivery pipeline — zero consumer code, delivers to fixed destinations (S3, Redshift, OpenSearch), buffers (60-900s) before delivery, includes Lambda transform and format conversion. Streams = flexibility. Firehose = simplicity.
questions.drawbacks: Firehose has delivery latency (buffered) — not suitable for true real-time. Streams require shard management.
questions.solutions: Use Firehose for standard S3 delivery use cases. Use Streams when you need real-time processing, multiple consumers, or custom logic.

questions.question: How does Amazon Athena optimize query cost and performance?
questions.answer: Athena charges $5/TB scanned. Optimization: (1) Columnar formats (Parquet/ORC) — queries only needed columns, reducing scan by 70-85%. (2) Partition pruning — data partitioned by date/region, queries with WHERE date='2024-01-15' only scan one partition. (3) Compression (SNAPPY/ZSTD) — reduces bytes scanned. (4) Partition projection — avoid Glue catalog lookups for large partition counts. Together: CSV uncompressed = $5/TB. Parquet + SNAPPY + partitioning = $0.05/TB (99% savings).
questions.drawbacks: Athena cannot update existing data (no DML on S3 files). No indexes — always scans data blocks even with partitioning.
questions.solutions: Use Delta Lake or Iceberg on S3 for UPDATE/DELETE support. Use partition projection for high partition count tables to avoid Glue catalog overhead.

questions.question: What is AWS Lake Formation and how does it differ from IAM + S3 bucket policies?
questions.answer: IAM + S3 bucket policies control access at the bucket/prefix level — coarse-grained. Lake Formation adds fine-grained access control: column-level (hide PII columns), row-level (analysts only see their region's data), and cell-level security on top of the Glue Data Catalog. It intercepts queries from Athena, Redshift Spectrum, Glue, and EMR and applies access controls transparently. One permission model across all query services instead of managing permissions in each service separately.
questions.drawbacks: Lake Formation adds complexity on top of IAM. Not all services integrate with Lake Formation permissions (some bypass it).
questions.solutions: Use Lake Formation for enterprise data lakes with strict access requirements. For simple setups, IAM + S3 bucket policies are sufficient. Audit Lake Formation access via CloudTrail.

questions.question: Explain Redshift distribution styles and when to use each.
questions.answer: EVEN: round-robin distribution across all nodes — good for tables with no clear join key or when table is always joined with ALL tables. KEY: rows with same distribution key co-located on same node — eliminates network shuffle for joins on that key. Use for large fact tables joined to large dimension tables. ALL: entire table copied to every node — no join shuffle needed. Use for small dimension tables (< 1M rows) frequently joined to fact tables. AUTO: Redshift chooses best style. For data engineering: use KEY on the most common join column (customer_id, order_id) for fact tables.
questions.drawbacks: Wrong distribution key causes data skew — one node holds 80% of data while others are nearly empty.
questions.solutions: Use EXPLAIN on queries to check for DS_DIST (redistribution) steps. Use table_inspector or SVL_QLOG to identify skewed tables. Rebalance with ALTER TABLE.

questions.question: What is the difference between SQS Standard and FIFO queues?
questions.answer: Standard: nearly unlimited throughput, best-effort ordering (not guaranteed), at-least-once delivery (may deliver duplicates). FIFO: strict ordering (first-in-first-out), exactly-once processing, limited to 300 msg/s (3,000 with batching). Use Standard for: high-throughput workloads, tasks where ordering doesn't matter, idempotent consumers. Use FIFO for: financial transactions, order processing, any workflow where sequence matters.
questions.drawbacks: FIFO throughput limit (300 msg/s) can be a bottleneck. Standard duplicates require idempotent processing.
questions.solutions: For FIFO at higher throughput, use message group IDs to parallelize (different groups processed in parallel). For Standard, implement idempotency using DynamoDB conditional writes.

questions.question: How does Lambda pricing work and what are cold starts?
questions.answer: Lambda pricing: (1) Requests: $0.20 per 1M requests (first 1M free). (2) Compute: $0.0000166667 per GB-second (memory × duration). Example: 128MB function running 200ms = 0.128 × 0.2 = 0.0256 GB-seconds. Cold start: when Lambda creates a NEW execution environment — downloads code, starts runtime, initializes dependencies. Takes 100ms-3s depending on runtime and memory. Warm start: reuses existing environment — no initialization overhead, ~1ms overhead.
questions.drawbacks: Cold starts add latency for latency-sensitive applications. Large deployment packages increase cold start time.
questions.solutions: Use Provisioned Concurrency to pre-warm environments. Minimize package size. Use Python/Node.js (faster cold starts than Java/C#). Keep Lambda warm with scheduled pings for non-provisioned functions.

questions.question: What is the Shared Responsibility Model in AWS?
questions.answer: AWS manages security OF the cloud: physical data centers, hardware, global network infrastructure, managed service software (RDS DB patching, Lambda runtime). Customer manages security IN the cloud: IAM permissions, security groups, encryption settings, OS patching on EC2, application code security, data classification and encryption. For managed services, AWS takes more responsibility: RDS — AWS patches OS and DB engine. For EC2 — customer patches OS. For Lambda — AWS manages runtime patching.
questions.drawbacks: Customers often assume AWS handles everything. Misconfigured S3 bucket policies, overly permissive IAM roles, and unpatched EC2 instances are customer responsibility.
questions.solutions: Use AWS Config rules to detect misconfigurations. Enable GuardDuty for threat detection. Use Security Hub for centralized compliance view. Regular IAM permission audits.

questions.question: Explain the difference between CloudWatch and CloudTrail.
questions.answer: CloudWatch: performance monitoring and alerting. Collects metrics (CPU, memory, latency, errors), logs (application and service logs), sets alarms, creates dashboards. Answers: 'Is my system healthy? How is it performing?' CloudTrail: security and compliance audit log. Records every API call made to AWS — who, what, when, from where. Answers: 'Who deleted that S3 bucket? Who changed that IAM policy?' CloudWatch tells you WHAT is happening to your resources. CloudTrail tells you WHO made changes.
questions.drawbacks: CloudTrail data events (S3 object-level, Lambda invocations) are extra cost and can generate massive volume.
questions.solutions: Enable CloudTrail in all regions with a multi-region trail. Store logs in S3 with integrity validation. Use Athena to query CloudTrail logs for investigations.

questions.question: What is AWS DMS and how does it enable near-zero-downtime migration?
questions.answer: AWS DMS (Database Migration Service) migrates databases with minimal downtime using full-load + CDC. Process: (1) Start CDC replication — DMS reads transaction logs from source. (2) Full load — migrate all existing data while CDC captures concurrent changes. (3) Apply CDC changes — apply all changes that occurred during full load. (4) Monitor lag — when source and target are nearly in sync (< 1 second lag). (5) Cutover — switch application connection string during low-traffic window. Downtime = only the cutover switch (~minutes).
questions.drawbacks: Schema transformation is limited — heterogeneous migrations (Oracle→PostgreSQL) may need manual schema conversion. Large LOB columns slow replication.
questions.solutions: Use AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) for heterogeneous schema conversion. Split large LOB column tables into separate DMS tasks.

questions.question: How does Auto Scaling work in AWS and what are the different scaling policies?
questions.answer: Auto Scaling Groups maintain desired capacity of EC2 instances. Policies: (1) Target Tracking: simplest — maintain a metric at target (CPU at 60%). AWS automatically adds/removes instances. (2) Step Scaling: different scaling actions based on alarm breach size (CPU 60-80%: add 1, CPU 80-100%: add 3). (3) Scheduled: pre-scale at known times (Mondays 8am scale up, 8pm scale down). (4) Predictive: ML-based forecast — proactively scales before expected load.
questions.drawbacks: Scale-in can terminate instances with active requests. Scale-out has a warmup period where new instances aren't ready.
questions.solutions: Configure instance warm-up period. Use connection draining on ELB (de-register gracefully before termination). Combine Predictive + Target Tracking for best results.

questions.question: What is Amazon Aurora and how does it differ from standard RDS?
questions.answer: Aurora is AWS's proprietary MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible database. Key differences: Storage auto-scales from 10GB to 128TB (no pre-provisioning). 6 copies of data across 3 AZs (2 per AZ) — self-healing. Up to 15 low-latency read replicas sharing the same storage (< 10ms replication lag vs seconds for RDS). Cluster endpoint for writes, reader endpoint for reads. Up to 5x MySQL and 3x PostgreSQL performance. Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scales compute. Backtrack (rewind without restore).
questions.drawbacks: 20-30% more expensive than standard RDS. Aurora-specific features create some AWS lock-in.
questions.solutions: Cost is offset by needing fewer/smaller instances for same workload. MySQL/PostgreSQL compatibility minimizes application code lock-in.

questions.question: Explain the difference between ElastiCache Redis and Memcached.
questions.answer: Redis: rich data structures (strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes, streams), optional persistence (RDB snapshots + AOF), replication (master-replica), pub/sub, Lua scripting, multi-AZ with failover, Redis Cluster for sharding. Memcached: simple key-value only, no persistence, no replication, pure cache — data lost on restart. Choose Redis for: sessions, leaderboards, real-time analytics, message queues, any use case needing persistence or replication. Choose Memcached for: simple cache layer, maximum throughput, multi-threaded performance.
questions.drawbacks: Redis single-threaded for commands (though I/O is async). Memcached has no persistence.
questions.solutions: Use Redis for almost all new deployments — richer features at similar cost. Use Memcached only when benchmarks show it's faster for your specific workload.

questions.question: What is AWS Step Functions and when would you use it over Airflow?
questions.answer: Step Functions: serverless state machine orchestrator for AWS services. Define workflows in JSON/YAML (ASL). Native integration with 200+ AWS services without writing code (direct SDK integrations). No infrastructure to manage. Scales instantly. Best for: AWS-native workflows, microservice coordination, human approval workflows, parallel processing of AWS tasks. Airflow: Python-based, general-purpose orchestrator. Better for: complex scheduling (cron-based), data pipeline DAGs, external system integrations, when team knows Python.
questions.drawbacks: Step Functions ASL (Amazon States Language) JSON is verbose and complex. Debugging nested state machines is harder than Airflow DAGs. More expensive for high-frequency executions.
questions.solutions: Use Step Functions for AWS service orchestration and event-driven workflows. Use Airflow (MWAA) for complex data pipeline scheduling with external integrations.

questions.question: How do you design a data lake on AWS?
questions.answer: Three-zone S3 structure: (1) Raw/Bronze zone: exact copy of source data, never modified, partitioned by ingestion date. (2) Silver/Cleansed zone: cleaned, typed, deduplicated Parquet files, partitioned by business date. (3) Gold/Curated zone: aggregated, domain-specific, ready for BI. Glue Data Catalog for all metadata. Lake Formation for access control. Glue/EMR for ETL. Athena for ad-hoc queries. Kinesis Firehose for streaming ingestion. CloudTrail + Lake Formation audit logs for governance. MWAA (Airflow) for orchestration.
questions.drawbacks: Without governance, data lake becomes a data swamp — untrustworthy, undiscoverable data.
questions.solutions: Implement data catalog from day one. Enforce data quality checks at each zone transition. Use data contracts. Tag all S3 objects with owner and sensitivity.

questions.question: What is DynamoDB's consistency model?
questions.answer: DynamoDB offers two read consistency options: (1) Eventually Consistent Reads (default): reads may reflect slightly stale data (within 1 second of a write) — all 3 storage nodes may not be in sync yet. Half the read capacity cost. (2) Strongly Consistent Reads: always returns the most up-to-date data. Reads from the leader node. Full read capacity cost. Use eventually consistent for most reads (dashboards, analytics). Use strongly consistent for financial records, inventory checks, any use case where stale data causes business issues.
questions.drawbacks: Strongly consistent reads cost 2x read capacity units and have higher latency.
questions.solutions: Default to eventually consistent. Explicitly request strongly consistent only when data freshness is critical.

questions.question: How does CloudFront improve performance and reduce costs?
questions.answer: CloudFront caches content at 400+ edge locations worldwide, serving requests from the nearest edge to the user instead of the origin. Performance: reduces latency from 200ms (cross-continent origin request) to 5-20ms (edge cache hit). Cost reduction: (1) Reduces origin server load — edge serves cached responses. (2) S3 transfer: data transferred via CloudFront is cheaper than direct S3 transfer. (3) Shield Standard included free — DDoS protection without additional cost.
questions.drawbacks: Dynamic content cannot be cached (each user's personalized response is unique). Cache invalidation has cost ($0.005 per path for first 1,000 paths/month).
questions.solutions: Separate static (images, JS, CSS — high cache TTL) from dynamic content (API responses — low/no cache). Use CloudFront Functions or Lambda@Edge for personalization at the edge without going to origin.

questions.question: What are Spot Instances and what are the best practices for using them?
questions.answer: Spot Instances use AWS spare EC2 capacity at up to 90% discount. AWS can reclaim them with 2-minute warning. Best practices: (1) Use diversified instance types and AZs in Spot Fleets — if one pool is interrupted, others continue. (2) Implement interruption handlers — poll EC2 metadata every 5 seconds for termination notice. (3) Checkpoint work to S3 so jobs resume from checkpoint on new instance. (4) Use Spot for stateless, fault-tolerant workloads: Spark task nodes, ML training, batch processing, CI/CD. (5) Use On-Demand for master/primary nodes.
questions.drawbacks: Interruption causes job failure if not handled. Some Spot pools have low availability in certain regions/instance types.
questions.solutions: Use Spot with On-Demand fallback in EC2 Fleet. Enable checkpointing in Spark Structured Streaming. Use EMR with multiple instance types for Spot pool diversity.

questions.question: What is the difference between CloudFormation and CDK?
questions.answer: CloudFormation: infrastructure defined in YAML/JSON templates. Declarative — describe desired state. AWS deploys resources. Learning curve for YAML syntax. Limited reuse (nested stacks). CDK: write infrastructure in Python, TypeScript, Java, Go. CDK synthesizes to CloudFormation templates. Benefits: real programming language (loops, conditions, functions), higher-level abstractions (constructs), reusable libraries (npm/pip packages), testing with standard test frameworks. Same CloudFormation underneath — CDK is an abstraction layer.
questions.drawbacks: CDK requires programming knowledge. Synthesized templates can be very large and hard to debug. CDK version upgrades can break existing stacks.
questions.solutions: Use CDK for complex infrastructure with repeated patterns. Use CloudFormation directly for simple stacks or teams without programming background.

questions.question: How does AWS KMS work and what is envelope encryption?
questions.answer: KMS manages encryption keys using HSMs. Direct encryption with KMS: limited to 4KB data. Envelope encryption: (1) KMS generates a data encryption key (DEK) — returns plaintext DEK + encrypted DEK. (2) Use plaintext DEK to encrypt your large data with AES-256. (3) Store encrypted DEK with the encrypted data. (4) Discard plaintext DEK from memory. To decrypt: call KMS to decrypt the encrypted DEK → use plaintext DEK to decrypt data. This is how S3 SSE-KMS and EBS encryption work. KMS only needs to protect the small DEK, not the large data.
questions.drawbacks: KMS has API rate limits (10,000 requests/second per region by default). High S3 request rates can hit KMS throttling.
questions.solutions: Enable S3 Bucket Keys to reduce KMS API calls by 99% (bucket-level key instead of per-object). Request KMS quota increases for high-throughput workloads.

questions.question: What are EC2 placement groups and when would you use them?
questions.answer: Placement groups control physical placement of instances: (1) Cluster: all instances on same rack/AZ. Very low network latency (10Gbps+), high bandwidth. Risk: single rack failure takes all instances. Use for: HPC, tightly-coupled distributed computing, low-latency data processing. (2) Spread: each instance on different hardware. Maximum fault tolerance. Max 7 instances per AZ. Use for: small critical applications where each instance must survive hardware failure. (3) Partition: groups of instances in separate partitions (separate racks). For distributed systems: Kafka, Cassandra, HDFS — each replica partition on different hardware.
questions.drawbacks: Cluster placement group: single rack failure = entire group down. Cannot add certain instance types to existing cluster group.
questions.solutions: Use cluster placement for compute-intensive workloads. Use partition for distributed databases. Reserve instances in cluster groups to ensure capacity.

questions.question: Explain how EMR differs from Glue for Spark processing.
questions.answer: AWS Glue: fully serverless Spark. No cluster to manage. Cold start 1-3 minutes. Limited Spark version (AWS-managed). Best for: simple-medium ETL, teams wanting zero infra management, infrequent jobs. EMR: EC2-based Spark cluster. Full control over Spark version, configuration, custom libraries. Pre-warmed clusters (no cold start). Supports YARN/Kubernetes. Best for: complex Spark tuning, latest Spark features, high-frequency jobs (amortize startup cost), tight integration with Hadoop ecosystem. EMR Serverless: serverless Spark like Glue but with full Spark control.
questions.drawbacks: Glue: limited Spark version, DPU pricing expensive for small jobs. EMR: cluster management overhead, idle cluster costs money.
questions.solutions: Use Glue for simple ETL and event-driven pipelines. Use EMR (or EMR Serverless) for complex Spark jobs needing tuning.

questions.question: What is Amazon EventBridge and how does it enable event-driven architectures?
questions.answer: EventBridge is a serverless event bus that routes events between producers and consumers based on content-based filtering. It receives events from AWS services (EC2 state changes, S3 uploads, RDS snapshots), SaaS applications (Salesforce, Zendesk), and custom applications. Rules filter events by pattern matching (source, detail-type, field values) and route to targets (Lambda, SQS, SNS, ECS, Step Functions, Kinesis, API Gateway). Decouples producers from consumers — producer doesn't know about consumers.
questions.drawbacks: EventBridge guarantees at-least-once delivery — consumer must be idempotent. Event schema evolution is not enforced — producers can change event format silently.
questions.solutions: Use EventBridge Schema Registry to document and validate event schemas. Implement idempotent consumers using DynamoDB conditional writes. Use DLQ on targets.

questions.question: How would you design a cost-optimized data lake on AWS?
questions.answer: Cost optimization layers: (1) Storage: use lifecycle policies — Standard (0-30 days) → Standard-IA → Glacier → Deep Archive. Use Parquet + SNAPPY/ZSTD compression (10x size reduction vs CSV). (2) Compute: EMR Spot instances for batch processing (90% savings). Use Glue for serverless ETL. AWS Batch with Spot for data processing jobs. (3) Query: Athena on Parquet+partitioned data ($0.05/query vs $5). Use Athena result reuse caching. (4) Networking: S3 VPC Gateway Endpoint (eliminates NAT Gateway data charges). (5) Reserved capacity: Savings Plans for steady-state Redshift/EMR.
questions.drawbacks: Spot interruptions can cause job failures. Glacier retrieval costs negate savings if data accessed frequently.
questions.solutions: Use S3 Intelligent-Tiering for uncertain access patterns. Implement Spark checkpointing for Spot resilience. Model retrieval frequency before moving to Glacier.

questions.question: What is Redshift Spectrum and when would you use it?
questions.answer: Redshift Spectrum runs SQL queries directly on S3 data without loading it into Redshift. Uses Glue Data Catalog for schema. Query joins data from Redshift tables and S3 simultaneously. Use cases: query historical data in S3 without paying to store it in Redshift, federated queries across hot (Redshift) and cold (S3) data, run ad-hoc queries on raw data before deciding to load it. Data stays in S3 — no duplication costs.
questions.drawbacks: Spectrum is slower than native Redshift queries on the same data (S3 I/O vs local SSD). Spectrum charges $5/TB scanned (same as Athena).
questions.solutions: Use columnar Parquet + partitioning for Spectrum queries. Load frequently-accessed data into Redshift tables. Use Spectrum for historical/cold data access.

questions.question: How does AWS handle encryption at rest vs encryption in transit?
questions.answer: At rest: AWS services offer server-side encryption (SSE) using KMS keys. S3: SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C. EBS: AES-256 via KMS. RDS: TDE and KMS. DynamoDB: KMS. Redshift: KMS. Most services now encrypt by default. In transit: TLS 1.2/1.3 for all AWS API calls. S3: enforce HTTPS via bucket policy (deny if not aws:SecureTransport). RDS/Aurora: enforce SSL via parameter group. Kinesis: TLS. MSK: TLS + client authentication (mTLS).
questions.drawbacks: Encryption at rest using KMS has API rate limits. Client applications must be configured to verify TLS certificates.
questions.solutions: Use S3 Bucket Keys to reduce KMS API calls by 99%. Enable aws:SecureTransport condition in bucket policies. Monitor TLS deprecation alerts in Security Hub.

questions.question: What is AWS GuardDuty and how does it work?
questions.answer: GuardDuty is an ML-based threat detection service that continuously analyzes CloudTrail logs, VPC Flow Logs, DNS logs, and Kubernetes audit logs without agents or additional infrastructure. It detects: cryptocurrency mining on EC2, compromised credentials (unusual API calls from TOR), data exfiltration (large S3 downloads from unknown IPs), malware communication (C&C server connections), and container escapes. Outputs findings to Security Hub, EventBridge (trigger Lambda for auto-remediation), or directly.
questions.drawbacks: GuardDuty generates alerts but doesn't block threats automatically. Alert fatigue if not tuned.
questions.solutions: Integrate with EventBridge to trigger Lambda for automated remediation (isolate compromised instance, revoke credentials). Use Trusted IP lists and Suppression rules to reduce false positives.

questions.question: Explain the difference between SNS and SQS.
questions.answer: SQS: queue — one message delivered to ONE consumer. Messages persist until consumed (up to 14 days). Pull model — consumer polls for messages. Decouples producer from consumer speed. SNS: topic — one message published to MANY subscribers simultaneously (fan-out). Push model — SNS pushes to all subscribers. Subscribers: Lambda, SQS, email, SMS, HTTPS, mobile push. Messages not persisted — if subscriber fails, message lost. Common pattern: SNS fan-out to multiple SQS queues (SNS → multiple SQS queues → multiple consumer groups, each with persistence and retry).
questions.drawbacks: SNS: no persistence, no ordering. SQS: each message consumed by only one consumer (not for fan-out).
questions.solutions: Use SNS→SQS fan-out pattern for durable, fan-out messaging. Use EventBridge for complex routing with content-based filtering.

questions.question: How do you implement disaster recovery on AWS?
questions.answer: Four DR strategies (by RTO/RPO and cost): (1) Backup & Restore: cheapest. Backup to S3/Glacier. Restore on disaster (hours RTO). (2) Pilot Light: minimal infrastructure running in DR region (DB replicated, AMIs available). Scale up on disaster (tens of minutes). (3) Warm Standby: scaled-down but functional environment running in DR region. Scale up on disaster (minutes). (4) Active-Active (Multi-Site): full capacity in multiple regions, Route 53 health checks route traffic. Near-zero RTO/RPO but 2x cost.
questions.drawbacks: Active-Active requires application-level conflict resolution for multi-region writes. Data replication lag creates inconsistency window.
questions.solutions: Use Route 53 health checks for automatic DNS failover. Use DynamoDB Global Tables for multi-region active-active DB. Use S3 cross-region replication for data. Test DR regularly (Game Days).

questions.question: What is AWS Macie and how does it help with data governance?
questions.answer: Amazon Macie uses ML to automatically discover, classify, and protect sensitive data in S3. It identifies PII (names, credit card numbers, SSNs, phone numbers, email addresses), credentials, and custom data patterns across all S3 buckets. Generates findings for: unencrypted buckets, publicly accessible buckets, buckets shared with external accounts, and sensitive data in unexpected locations. Essential for GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS compliance.
questions.drawbacks: Macie charges per GB scanned — large data lakes can be expensive. Some false positives in custom pattern matching.
questions.solutions: Run Macie on new data and changes only. Use findings to classify existing S3 data with tags. Integrate Macie findings with Security Hub for centralized governance.

questions.question: How does AWS Batch differ from Lambda for batch processing?
questions.answer: Lambda: max 15 minutes execution, max 10GB memory, max 10GB storage, serverless but limited. AWS Batch: no time limit, EC2/Fargate-based, any Docker container, full compute resources (TB RAM, hundreds of vCPUs, GPU). Manages queuing, scheduling, retries automatically. Optimizes for cost using Spot instances. Use Lambda for: event-driven, short-duration, lightweight processing. Use Batch for: long-running jobs, large memory requirements, ML training, genome sequencing, rendering, financial simulations.
questions.drawbacks: Batch job startup overhead (EC2 provisioning). Spot interruptions require job restart logic.
questions.solutions: Use Batch checkpointing to resume interrupted Spot jobs. Use Fargate launch type for Batch to avoid EC2 management.

questions.question: What is AWS Transit Gateway and when do you need it?
questions.answer: Transit Gateway is a central hub connecting multiple VPCs and on-premises networks. Without TGW: N VPCs need N*(N-1)/2 peering connections (10 VPCs = 45 peerings). With TGW: N VPCs = N attachments (10 VPCs = 10 connections). TGW supports: VPC attachments, VPN connections, Direct Connect gateways, cross-region peering. Use when: managing 5+ VPCs that need to communicate, hub-and-spoke network topology, connecting on-premises to multiple VPCs, centralized egress/inspection.
questions.drawbacks: $0.05/hour per attachment + $0.02/GB data transfer. For 2-3 VPCs, VPC Peering is cheaper.
questions.solutions: Use VPC Peering for simple 2-3 VPC connectivity. Use Transit Gateway for complex multi-account, multi-VPC architectures.

questions.question: What is Amazon Bedrock and how does it differ from SageMaker?
questions.answer: Bedrock: managed service providing access to pre-built foundation models (Claude, Titan, Llama, Mistral) via simple API. Zero ML expertise needed — just call the API. Also provides Agents (autonomous task completion) and Knowledge Bases (RAG). SageMaker: full ML platform for building, training, and deploying custom ML models. Requires ML expertise, data preparation, model training, evaluation, deployment. Use Bedrock for: LLM-powered applications, chatbots, summarization, generation, RAG — using existing FMs. Use SageMaker for: custom model training, specialized models, fine-tuning on your data.
questions.drawbacks: Bedrock: limited model customization, vendor-selected models. SageMaker: requires ML expertise, complex setup.
questions.solutions: Use Bedrock for most generative AI use cases. Use SageMaker fine-tuning on Bedrock models when generic models underperform. Use SageMaker for traditional ML (tabular data, recommendation systems).

questions.question: How do you secure data in transit between services in AWS?
questions.answer: Multiple layers: (1) TLS 1.2/1.3 for all AWS API calls (enforced by AWS). (2) S3: enforce HTTPS via bucket policy (Deny if aws:SecureTransport is false). (3) RDS/Aurora: SSL/TLS required parameter (rds.force_ssl=1). Verify certificate in connection string. (4) Kinesis: TLS encryption. (5) MSK: TLS + optional mTLS for client authentication. (6) VPC: private subnets + VPC Endpoints prevent traffic leaving AWS network. (7) PrivateLink for service-to-service communication without internet.
questions.drawbacks: Older applications may not support TLS 1.2+. Certificate validation errors break connections.
questions.solutions: Update older DB drivers to TLS-compatible versions. Use ACM (Certificate Manager) for free TLS certificates. Enable Security Hub TLS deprecation findings.

questions.question: What is Amazon MSK and when would you choose it over Kinesis?
questions.answer: MSK (Managed Kafka): managed Apache Kafka. Standard Kafka API — works with any Kafka client library. Portable — same code works on-premises, other clouds. Full Kafka ecosystem: Connect, Streams, ksqlDB. Choose MSK when: migrating existing Kafka workloads, multi-cloud or hybrid setup, need full Kafka ecosystem, team has Kafka expertise, high throughput (>500MB/s). Choose Kinesis when: AWS-only, simpler managed experience, native AWS service integration, cost-sensitive at lower throughput.
questions.drawbacks: MSK: must manage broker capacity (choose instance types and count). More expensive than Kinesis at lower throughput. MSK Serverless available but more expensive per-use.
questions.solutions: MSK Serverless for variable workloads. Provision MSK brokers for predictable high-throughput. Use Kinesis for simple AWS-native streaming.

questions.question: How does AWS handle multi-region high availability?
questions.answer: Multi-region HA components: Route 53 with latency or failover routing + health checks for DNS-level failover. S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) for data durability. RDS Aurora Global Database for < 1 second cross-region replication. DynamoDB Global Tables for active-active multi-region DB. CloudFront for global edge caching. EC2 with AMI copying across regions. Full active-active: traffic split across regions via Route 53 latency routing, each region handles its own users. DR: primary region with secondary region on standby, Route 53 failover routing activates secondary on health check failure.
questions.drawbacks: Active-active multi-region requires conflict resolution for database writes. Significantly increases cost and complexity.
questions.solutions: For most workloads, Multi-AZ within one region is sufficient. Add cross-region DR only when RTO/RPO business requirements demand it. Use DynamoDB Global Tables for global active-active apps.

questions.question: What is ECS vs EKS and when would you choose each?
questions.answer: ECS: AWS-native container orchestration. Simpler — no Kubernetes knowledge needed. Deep AWS integration (ALB, IAM, CloudWatch, Secrets Manager all native). Fargate launch type = fully serverless. EKS: managed Kubernetes control plane. Standard Kubernetes API — portable, large ecosystem. More powerful features (custom schedulers, operators, Helm charts). Steeper learning curve. Choose ECS when: team new to containers, AWS-only deployment, want simplicity. Choose EKS when: Kubernetes expertise exists, need Kubernetes-specific features (operators, CRDs), multi-cloud portability, complex scheduling.
questions.drawbacks: ECS: AWS lock-in, less ecosystem. EKS: complexity, Kubernetes operational overhead even with managed control plane.
questions.solutions: Start with ECS for simplicity. Migrate to EKS when Kubernetes-specific features are needed. Use Fargate with both to eliminate node management.

questions.question: How do Reserved Instances and Savings Plans differ?
questions.answer: Reserved Instances: commit to specific instance type, region, OS, tenancy. Standard RI (biggest discount, no changes). Convertible RI (can change family/OS/AZ, less discount). 1 or 3 year terms. Savings Plans: commit to dollar amount of compute usage per hour. More flexible. Compute Savings Plan: applies to EC2 any family/region, Lambda, Fargate. EC2 Instance SP: specific family/region, biggest discount. Same or better discounts vs RI with more flexibility. Choose Savings Plans for most use cases. Use Standard RI only for very stable, unchanging workloads.
questions.drawbacks: Commitment risk: if workload changes or instance type deprecated, you pay for unused capacity.
questions.solutions: Use Cost Explorer RI/SP recommendations. Start with 1-year term. Use Convertible RI or Compute SP for flexibility. Sell unused RIs on AWS Marketplace.

questions.question: Explain how you would implement a real-time streaming data pipeline on AWS.
questions.answer: Architecture: (1) Producers: applications write events to Kinesis Data Streams or MSK. (2) Stream processing: Kinesis Data Analytics (Managed Flink) reads streams, applies transformations, windowed aggregations, joins. (3) Real-time serving: results written to DynamoDB (low-latency reads), ElastiCache Redis (dashboards), or OpenSearch (search/analytics). (4) Historical storage: Kinesis Firehose parallel-delivers to S3 (Parquet, partitioned) for batch analytics. (5) Alerting: Flink detects anomalies → EventBridge → SNS → Slack/PagerDuty. (6) Orchestration: Managed Flink applications run continuously, monitored via CloudWatch.
questions.drawbacks: Managing stateful Flink applications requires understanding checkpointing, watermarks. Schema evolution breaks consumers.
questions.solutions: Use Schema Registry (MSK or Glue) for schema governance. Enable Flink checkpointing to S3 every 60 seconds. Use Kinesis Firehose in parallel for archival to avoid data loss.

questions.question: What is S3 Versioning and when would you enable it?
questions.answer: S3 Versioning maintains multiple versions of an object. Every PUT creates a new version. DELETE adds a delete marker (previous versions still exist). Enable for: data lake raw zone (protect against accidental overwrites/deletes), audit compliance, state management for ML experiments, configuration file backups. Versioning + MFA Delete provides strongest protection against accidental deletion.
questions.drawbacks: Versioning increases storage costs — all versions of every object are stored. Listing objects in versioned buckets returns all versions by default.
questions.solutions: Combine versioning with lifecycle policies to expire non-current versions after N days (balance protection vs cost). Separate versioned buckets for raw data from non-versioned buckets for processed data.

questions.question: How does IAM Permission Boundary work?
questions.answer: Permission Boundary sets the maximum permissions an IAM identity can have. Even if a policy grants s3:*, the permission boundary limits actual effective permissions. Use case: safely delegate IAM permission management — let a developer create IAM roles for their applications, but limit those roles to never exceed certain permissions. The role can have at most what the boundary allows, regardless of what policies are attached. Effective permissions = intersection of identity-based policies AND permission boundary.
questions.drawbacks: Permission boundaries add complexity. Forgetting to set them makes delegation unsafe.
questions.solutions: Always attach permission boundary when delegating IAM role creation. Use AWS Organizations SCPs for account-level controls. Automate boundary attachment in IAM role creation automation.

questions.question: What is Athena Federated Query and how does it work?
questions.answer: Athena Federated Query allows running SQL queries across multiple data sources beyond S3 — including RDS, DynamoDB, ElastiCache, OpenSearch, HBase, and custom sources. Uses Lambda-based Data Source Connectors as the translation layer. Athena sends subqueries to Lambda connectors, which fetch data from the source. Results joined in Athena. All through standard SQL. Use for: single-pane analytics across operational DBs and data lake, avoid ETL for low-frequency queries, query real-time operational data alongside historical S3 data.
questions.drawbacks: Federated queries are slower than native Athena (Lambda cold start, cross-source network calls). Not suitable for high-frequency or latency-sensitive queries.
questions.solutions: Use federated queries for ad-hoc analysis. For frequent queries, implement proper ETL to bring data into S3/Redshift instead of repeated federated access.

questions.question: How do you monitor and alert on AWS infrastructure?
questions.answer: Four-layer monitoring: (1) Metrics: CloudWatch collects all AWS service metrics automatically. Create alarms on critical metrics (Lambda errors, RDS CPU, Kinesis iterator age). (2) Logs: CloudWatch Logs aggregates all service and application logs. CloudWatch Log Insights for SQL-like querying. (3) Traces: X-Ray for distributed request tracing across microservices. (4) Events: CloudTrail for API audit. EventBridge for event-driven alerting. Alert routing: CloudWatch Alarms → SNS → email/Slack/PagerDuty. Use Composite Alarms to reduce alert noise (alarm fires only when multiple metrics breach simultaneously).
questions.drawbacks: Default CloudWatch resolution is 1 minute — may miss sub-minute spikes. Alert fatigue from too many low-severity alarms.
questions.solutions: Use 1-second High-Resolution Metrics for latency-sensitive monitoring. Implement alarm hierarchies (warning → critical). Use CloudWatch Anomaly Detection for dynamic thresholds.

questions.question: Design a complete data platform architecture on AWS for a mid-size company.
questions.answer: Architecture: Ingestion: DMS CDC from RDS PostgreSQL → MSK. Kinesis Firehose for application events → S3 Bronze. AppFlow for Salesforce/SaaS → S3. Storage: S3 with three zones (Bronze/Silver/Gold) + Glue Data Catalog + Lake Formation for access control. Processing: MWAA (Airflow) for orchestration. Glue Spark for batch ETL (Bronze→Silver→Gold). Managed Flink for real-time aggregations. Serving: Redshift with Spectrum for complex analytics. Athena for ad-hoc queries. QuickSight for BI dashboards. SageMaker for ML. Governance: Lake Formation column/row security. KMS CMK for encryption. Macie for PII detection. CloudTrail + Glue audit logs. Operations: CloudWatch + X-Ray for monitoring. EventBridge for alerting. Multi-account: Dev, Staging, Prod accounts with separate data lakes. Cost optimization: Spot EMR/Batch, S3 lifecycle policies, Savings Plans for Redshift.
questions.drawbacks: Complexity — 15+ services to manage, integrate, and monitor. IAM role proliferation across services.
questions.solutions: Start simple (S3 + Glue + Athena + QuickSight). Add services as needed. Use IaC (CDK) for all infrastructure. Tag everything for cost allocation. Use AWS Well-Architected Framework reviews quarterly.
